title: MiFID II Algo Trading — Per-Instrument Circuit Breaker Trigger Frequency and Cooling-Off Compliance
exclusions: Excludes test/sandbox environments, pre-production systems, development instances without regulated data, and any systems formally documented as out-of-scope in the MiFID II programme scope statement approved by the control owner.
evidence: Saved search results archived to the `audit_evidence` index with MiFID II control tagging; scheduled PDF/CSV exports for auditor consumption; Splunk dashboard screenshots with timestamp watermarks capturing control operating effectiveness over the review period.

compliance.assurance_rationale: MiFID II Art.17 — UC provides continuous Splunk telemetry evidence for this regulatory requirement, uplifted from contributing to partial after review confirmed the SPL captures relevant monitoring signals.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that MiFID II Art.17 (Algorithmic trading controls) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.5.9: MiFID II Algo Trading — Per-Instrument Circuit Breaker Trigger Frequency and Cooling-Off Compliance.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.5.9' running on index=trading sourcetype="oms:circuit" (algo_id, symbol, venue, trigger_ts, breaker_type, cooldown_until, resumed_ts), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Inject a synthetic test event that satisfies the detection criteria for 'MiFID II Algo Trading — Per-Instrument Circuit Breaker Trigg' — for example, create a log entry with all required fields populated to match the SPL filter conditions. Verify the search fires within the expected schedule interval, the alert populates the compliance dashboard, and the event routes correctly to the evidence index with proper MiFID II control tagging.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Ingest a batch of test events that are structurally similar but explicitly do NOT meet the detection threshold — e.g., events with compliant values, timestamps outside the monitoring window, or source systems excluded by the scope filter. Verify the search produces zero results, no false-positive alerts fire, and the compliance posture score remains unaffected for the MiFID II control objective.

dataSources: `index=trading` `sourcetype="oms:circuit"` (algo_id, symbol, venue, trigger_ts, breaker_type, cooldown_until, resumed_ts)
app: HTTP Event Collector from OMS/EMS, market access gateway logs (Splunkbase)
spl:
index=trading sourcetype="oms:circuit" earliest=-90d
| eval cool=strptime(cooldown_until,"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ"), res=strptime(resumed_ts,"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ")
| where isnotnull(res) AND res<cool
| stats count as early_resumes by algo_id, venue, symbol
| sort - early_resumes
description: Circuit breakers protect market integrity. Monitoring how often firm algos hit exchange or broker breaker thresholds, and whether mandatory cooling-off periods were observed before re-enable, supports Art. 17 governance of algorithmic activity.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for MiFID II compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes trading and sourcetypes oms:circuit. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Normalize timestamps to UTC; (2) map `algo_id` to registered strategy in inventory; (3) escalate to compliance officer on any early resume; (4) exclude test venues via lookup; (5) retain for regulatory interview packs. Indexes required: `index=trading`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=oms:circuit`.
visualization: Table (violations), Timechart (triggers per symbol), Single value (early resumes).
knownFalsePositives:
1. **End-of-day batch reconciliation** — Automated nightly reconciliation processes between the order management system and the trade repository generate high-volume record comparison events that are a normal part of MiFID II best-execution reporting obligations.

2. **Market maker quoting activity** — Designated market makers operating under exchange agreements generate continuous quote streams and rapid order cancellations that are legitimate trading activity rather than market manipulation indicators.

3. **Corporate actions and settlement adjustments** — Position adjustments triggered by dividends, stock splits, mergers, or settlement instruction amendments create expected transaction pattern changes that do not indicate reporting failures.

4. **Regulatory reporting system maintenance** — Scheduled ARM/APA connectivity tests, reference data updates, and approved reporting system failovers produce transaction reporting gaps or anomalies that are operationally expected and pre-communicated to compliance.

**Suppression mechanism:** Maintain a `mifid_exception_register` KV Store lookup containing approved batch job identifiers, market maker firm codes, and scheduled maintenance windows. The search filters matches where `exception_type` is populated and within the valid exception time window.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Indexes**: `trading` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `Art.17`. The control objective is: "Evidence that MiFID II Art.17 (Algorithmic trading controls) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.5.9: MiFID II Algo Trading — Per-Instrument Circuit Breaker Trigger Frequency and Cooling-Off Compliance." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into `trading` under `oms:circuit`, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


5 operational considerations apply to the rollout of this detection:


• **Hint 1:** Normalize timestamps to UTC. Field normalisation mismatches are one of the top silent-failure modes in enrichment-driven detections — invest in automated tests that assert key-match rates after every TA upgrade.

• **Hint 2:** map `algo_id` to registered strategy in inventory. The register referenced here is primary evidence — treat changes to it with the same discipline as code changes.

• **Hint 3:** escalate to compliance officer on any early resume. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** exclude test venues via lookup. Exclusions should be time-bound and logged — a permanent exclusion baked into the search query becomes invisible over time and future owners cannot tell it was intentional.

• **Hint 5:** retain for regulatory interview packs. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

This search aggregates `count()` across the last 90 days, grouped by `algo_id, venue, symbol`. For "MiFID II Algo Trading — Per-Instrument Circuit Breaker Trigger Frequency and Cooling-Off Compliance," the aggregation produces the result shape required by the MiFID II control — each row is a summary record the regulation expects to be presentable at audit time. The aggregation approach is chosen to produce one result row per analytical unit, which is the typical requirement for compliance dashboarding and report generation.

Why aggregate at search time rather than rely on raw events: compliance reporting requires summarisation to be defensible and stable. A raw-event dashboard can show different counts depending on the exact second it was queried; an aggregated result rounded to the UC's schedule produces consistent numbers across the reporting period. If the downstream consumer (GRC platform, regulator submission) expects stable daily/weekly figures, schedule this search to write to a summary index and read from the summary for reports — that way the numbers quoted on Monday match what was quoted on Wednesday.

Cardinality vigilance: if `algo_id, venue, symbol` has very high cardinality (e.g. grouping by a free-text `user_agent` or `url` field), the result set explodes and the search becomes slow. Check cardinality with `| stats dc(algo_id) as cardinality` before scheduling; if >100000 distinct groups, either narrow the search, pre-filter to the top N values, or redesign the search to aggregate at a coarser grain.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.5.9: MiFID II Algo Trading — Per-Instrument Circuit Breaker Trigg]
cron_schedule = 0 3 1 * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -90d
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.5.9,reg=MiFID_II
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=trading sourcetype=oms:circuit earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `algo_id`, `cool`, `early_resumes`, `symbol`, `venue`. Run:

```spl
index=trading sourcetype=oms:circuit earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table algo_id, cool, early_resumes, symbol, venue
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.5.9: MiFID II evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Drill-through table (`violations`):** Full-width bottom row. Enable row-level drilldown to a secondary dashboard that expands each violation with its raw events, timeline, and related context. This is the panel the auditor will actually read — optimise it for scannability (avoid huge wrap-around fields).

**Panel 2 — Time trend (`triggers per symbol`):** Place in the main content row. Use `timechart span=1d` aggregated appropriately for the regulation's review cadence (daily for PCI/HIPAA, weekly for ISO-style controls). Annotate the chart with control-change dates so auditors can see when mitigations took effect.

**Panel 3 — Single-value KPI (`early resumes`):** Place at top-left. Configure `underLabel` with the regulation clause (e.g. 'MiFID II current violation count') and set a red colour range for any non-zero value. A zero reading is the steady state for a healthy control.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**Search returns no results when violations are expected:** Verify time-window alignment, index existence, and field extractions in that order. Most 'missing results' are simpler configuration issues before they're detection-logic issues.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**MiFID II-specific failure modes:**

• Best execution evidence (RTS 27/28): MiFID II requires venue-level best execution reporting. Detections must cover ALL execution venues the firm uses, not just the primary — a detection missing a single venue fails the 'all venues' requirement.

• Transaction reporting (MiFIR Art. 26): T+1 reporting to competent authority. The detection's latency must be tight enough to catch missing-report within the T+1 window; a 7-day cadence is regulatorily too slow.

• Pre-trade and post-trade transparency: pre-trade quotes and post-trade trade reports are separate obligations. Detections on transparency must differentiate; missing pre-trade quote != missing post-trade report.

• Systematic Internaliser thresholds: SI designation triggers at specific volume thresholds. Detections on SI obligations must read the latest SI thresholds (ESMA updates them periodically); a hardcoded threshold goes stale within months.
grandmaExplanation: We circuit breakers protect market integrity.
